title: Beer on Carpet
stainChemistry: Beer contains malt tannins, hop resins, proteins (from barley), and sugars. On carpet, the tannin component stains while the sugar creates a sticky residue that attracts dirt. Fresh beer stains are light and water-soluble. Dried beer stains darken as the tannins oxidize and the sugars caramelize, leaving a stiff, discolored patch.
whyThisWorks: Surfactant treatment dissolves the sugar and protein components on carpet. The acidic tannin spotter (professional) or oxidizer (DIY) targets the tannin discoloration. Beer stains are among the easier tannin stains because the tannin concentration is relatively low compared to wine or coffee.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden spot
homeSolutions.instruction: Find a hidden patch of the same carpet — inside a closet, under furniture, behind a door. Dab a drop of your cleaning solution (Dawn + water, or the peroxide mix from step 5) and blot with a white cloth after two minutes. If the carpet holds its color, you're safe to treat. If the cloth picks up dye, stop — carpet dye is unstable and home chemistry will lighten it. Go to step 9.

homeSolutions.agent: The tannin rule: no alkaline cleaners
homeSolutions.instruction: Beer stains are tannin, the same family as wine, coffee, and tea. Alkaline products — ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean, most "all-purpose" sprays — lock tannin into the fiber permanently and turn a pale beige ring into a dark brown one. Use only: cold water, dish soap, 3% hydrogen peroxide, and white vinegar. Nothing else.

homeSolutions.agent: Blot the spill immediately — never rub
homeSolutions.instruction: Press a clean white cloth or stack of paper towels straight down on the wet spot. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work from the outside of the stain inward so you don't spread it. Keep blotting until the cloth stops picking up beer. Rubbing mashes the beer and its sugars deeper into the pile and the pad beneath.

Pour a small amount of cold water directly on the stain to dilute any remaining beer, then blot immediately. Repeat two or three times. This matters especially if the spill sat for even a few minutes — the sugar in beer caramelizes as it dries and creates a sticky crust that attracts dirt. Water dissolves it before it sets.

Mix one tablespoon of clear Dawn dish soap with one cup of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a small bowl. Dip a white cloth in the solution, wring nearly dry, and dab the stain. Let it sit five minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Peroxide oxidizes the tannin color; Dawn lifts it and cuts the sugar. Repeat up to three cycles — most beer stains lift completely in this step.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with ammonia, bleach, or any other cleaner — dangerous gases. And test on your hidden patch first: peroxide can lighten bright carpet dyes.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar into one cup of cold water. Dab onto the treated spot with a white cloth. This does two things: it neutralizes any peroxide residue, and the mild acid keeps the tannin from darkening later. Blot dry with a clean cloth.

Blot the area until no more moisture transfers to a dry cloth. Stack six to ten sheets of white paper towels over the spot, weigh them down with a heavy book, and leave overnight. This pulls any residual beer up out of the carpet pad before it can wick back to the surface as a ring. Do not walk on the area or vacuum until fully dry.

Once dry, inspect the spot under good natural light — not lamplight. Beer rings often look gone when wet but reappear as a pale tan halo when dry. If you see one, repeat steps 5–7 a second time. Two full rounds is the home ceiling. If the ring still shows after that, escalate.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a professional carpet cleaner
homeSolutions.instruction: If two rounds of peroxide + vinegar didn't lift the stain, or if the carpet failed the patch test, call a pro carpet cleaner. Tell them: "Beer on [nylon/polyester/wool] carpet, treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide, vinegar rinse. Residual tan halo remains. No ammonia or alkaline products used." That handoff tells them you protected the tannin and lets them go straight to professional tannin spotter + extraction.

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No chlorine bleach on colored carpet
Avoid over-wetting — causes backing damage and mold
fiberSensitivities: Most synthetic carpet (nylon/polyester) tolerates standard chemistry
Always blot from outside in to prevent spreading
Extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

escalation.when: If beer stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is beer on carpet, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaning service with truck-mount extraction

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
